narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 28 (well under the usual 36.70), full-time enrollment plans, and middle family income bracket—and predicted an 82.7% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your admission, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. The three biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 decile ranking, and your LSAT score, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—meaning you ranked in the bottom tenth of your school that year—had the strongest negative impact, signaling to the model a significant drop in performance that often correlates with bar struggles. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1 reinforced this concern, as early low rankings like yours are rare compared to the average around the fifth or sixth decile and pushed the odds further toward failure. Your LSAT score of 28 also contributed notably in the same direction, standing out as lower than what's typical for students who pass the bar. Compared to average applicants, your academic deciles are at the very bottom of the distribution, which amplified these issues far more than your other solid traits like planning full-time study. While factors like your middle income aligned with the norm and didn't sway things much, they couldn't offset the top concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male student—many from similar backgrounds succeed with stronger early signals—but the model saw your profile as riskier than most. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses or a master's program, and building a standout personal statement highlighting resilience or relevant experiences. Reapplying after addressing these core academic gaps could shift your predicted odds dramatically, opening doors at schools that value growth stories like yours. Keep pushing; targeted improvements here have turned around predictions for many in your position.
